question_text: Hi how do I know if what I contribute to a article is actually valuable and won't get deleted?

If your edit does get deleted, or "reverted" as we call it on Wikipedia and you don't agree with it, you can ask the person who reverted it why they did it using their [POLICY]. If you want more info on how to add sources to articles, you can read the page [HELP_PAGE], and if you're still struggling, you can ask me for help. they/them •

Articles about people are sometimes removed if they don't have enough independent coverage. You can try checking the deletion log for the page to see the reason it was deleted.

question_text: I would like to edit the NSMV Wikipedia page ([LINK] The page does not contain the complete story surrounding the NSMV, has factual errors, and is out of date. My question is - how substantially can an existing page be edited? What happens if a page undergoes substantial changes/edits? Thanks for the help/advice!
reply_text: IWasInTheRoom It is usually best to break up large edits into multiple smaller ones. This makes it less likely that there is an [HELP_PAGE] and can be easier to actually implement the edits. Also, remember that all information added to an article has to be sourced from a [POLICY]. If you're ever unsure about something in the page, you can always ask about it on the talk page. If you have any other questions, you can ask me here or ask over at the [POLICY].

reply_text:
Hi ! Yes I am human :-)
I think fixing that [WIKILINK] link in [WIKILINK] could be a great first edit for you to make. There is a culture of fix it yourself around here. From the article's [WIKILINK] section, click [edit], then select Tobler's blue link. From there, you can either Edit the link to something like John Tobler (author), or if you think the author is highly unlikely to require a Wikipedia article, remove the link. This is an application of [POLICY].
That method is the VisualEditor. Alternatively you could click [edit source] and change the wikimarkup to [WIKILINK]. Either way it will look like [WIKILINK]; a red link ready for someone to create a new article. I think my explanation is as clear as mud but the documentation at [HELP_PAGE] doesn't seem much better. Just ask me if needed.
You can't break anything on Wikipedia and should not live in fear of bots. An amusing story is that when the first anti-vandalism bot was introduced back in about 2009 it reverted (essentially undid-ited) Wikipedia co-founder [WIKILINK] three times for a perfectly good edit. He was confused :-P. I see below you have figured out that any contributions you make, including to articles or talk pages, will be recorded in your user contributions.

question_text:
Hi I am actually just starting out, and I am helping out as a volunteer to edit someone's profile. I did however made a mistake in creating an initial account that matches her actual person (IreneNgAuthor). My thought process was actually to create it on-behalf for her and proceed to update the Wikipedia page and when the process is completed, handover the account to herself for ownership.
After reading her Wikipedia draft, I understand her intent (shifting her focus as an ex-politician to her current active role as a writer) and I started to (carelessly, using ChatGPT and her reference links which may have outdated) I have wasted the people's time reviewing information that has not been carefully vetted. For this I do feel ashamed and sorry for that.
Hence I started as a new account, that should matches the account policy. I too learned from the comments so far at [LINK] that it is not so much of what Ms Irene's objective but rather she is more well known as a politician in PAP for Singapore.
Hence I will like to ask for your advise how can I proceed to continue this update and also what should I do with the account IreneNgAuthor ?
reply_text: :Dancris7 Accounts cannot be shared and, as you learned, accounts created by someone else in the name of another person will quickly be blocked. Accounts cannot be deleted, so you can simply abandon the other account. For the new account, since you are editing on behalf of Ms. Ng, you must declare a conflict of interest per [POLICY]. The requested move (retitling of the article) has been denied, per the Talk page discussion: [WIKILINK]. Going forward, once you have declared your conflict of interest, you should not edit the article but instead propose changes on the article's Talk page. You should disclose your conflict of interest on the article's Talk page, as well as on your new account user page, so that the other volunteers are aware of the conflict as they review your proposed changes. Also, you know now that using an LLM like ChatGPT to edit Wikipedia is very highly discouraged.

question_text: Hi Estar8806, it is very good to have you as a mentor. reply_text: Hi @, That’s a great question and I’m so glad you asked. The best way to resolve content disputes before turning into to a complete [POLICY] is to discuss it with the other editor on their talk page. If there’s more than one other editor involved, try the article talk page. If you do start a discussion on the article talk page, you can ping the other editors using the “@“ symbol (ex. “@” is how someone would ping me). I hope this helps!

question_text: Hi, I created an account just now as I realised my IP address is being used to vandalise random and varied Wikipedia pages (most of which I’ve never visited before) and that my address has been repeatedly blocked from editing despite never having edited anything before. Since you have been able to edit from this account, you should probably be able to edit from it for the foreseeable future, though it is unlikely that your IP address itself will be unblocked. This is done in order to prevent abuse from the people who may be vandalizing from it. If you have any other questions about editing here, please do ask!

question_text:
I know you're busy - but my question is 'WHEN' to edit.
Should I always discuss changed in talk first? Should I run them past you first?
reply_text:
Hi there! I'm not your mentor, but I want to give my two cents. There's never, really, a bad time to edit. It can be a little daunting when you're new, but if you spot something you think could be fixed or revised, don't hesitate to [POLICY]! There's really no harm in making mistakes. You don't necessarily have to say anything on a talk page unless something is particularly controversial or if it's a bigger change you're afraid to make yourself quite yet. It's more of a notice board, really. I hope that makes sense? Talk pages are hard to explain. You can look at [POLICY] for more info on talk pages. Good luck out there and rock on!
Hi SoThatsCool and welcome to Wikipedia! ArkadenBoden is right. No bad time to edit; it's always a good time to edit. You don't need to run things by me. You don't have to ask for permission on a talkpage (with some exceptions as certain articles have a level of protection and that will be clearer stated once you try to edit that type of article). To help get you started, I left a with additional info for new editors.

question_text: Hi, I tried some basic copy editing for a first attempt. I came across this article where the book titles were in bold and there was inconsistent formatting. I wondered if it was okay to change to italics, or if there's a reason for things being in bold with different types of parenthesis?
reply_text: Hi @, text formatting on Wikipedia is specified in the [POLICY] guideline. On Wikipedia, the use of boldface is [POLICY]; the only common use of boldface is for the first appearance of the article title in the lead section. Italics are the preferred markup for emphasis (and only if necessary), titles of major works of art (books, films, etc.) and some other uses specified in the [POLICY] section of the aforementioned guideline. In this case it's probably appropriate to change the boldface to italics or remove formatting entirely if not required by guidelines.

question_text:
Hello Mx. Granger,
I had a few questions on what qualifies as a good article topics. I am an English and German speaking Wikipedia user living in Austria with an interest in geography. I have noticed there are many geographic features in my area that have articles on German Wikipedia, but are not present on English Wikipedia. For example there is a mountain in my city that has this article on de.wikipedia.org ([LINK] but no existing article exists on english wikipedia. The most mention Pfenningberg has in English Wikipedia is one bullet in the article for Linz.
Thank you in advance and apologies for the long message,
Glad Cow
reply_text:
Hi , thanks for the message! The basic guideline to determine what merits an article is the [POLICY] guideline. For geographic features there is also some guidance at [POLICY].
In general there are a lot of topics that are notable and have Wikipedia articles in other languages but still don't have articles in English, simply because no one has bothered to write one in English. In those cases, it's great if you can start an English-language article, either writing it from scratch based on reliable sources or translating from another language like German. I've done this kind of thing myself for geographical features like [WIKILINK], [WIKILINK], and [WIKILINK], which had articles in Chinese but not in English until I started them.
If you translate from a Wikipedia article in another language, make sure to provide attribution. A good practice is to mention it in your [POLICY] ("Translating from [WIKILINK]") and to use the template [TEMPLATE_REF] on the new article's talk page.
:Please let me know if any of that was unclear or if you have any other questions. — ( · )

question_text: I was thinking of making certain niche articles available in more languages (I speak several). Do I have to indicate new sources or can I reuse the same. E.g. if the original article is in Norwegian (with sources in Norwegian), can I translate it to German and still use the Norwegian-language sources?
reply_text: Hello! On the English Wikipedia, while English language sources are preferred as these are easier for English language speakers to verify the contents of, there are no language restrictions on the sources you use, so you are free to use sources from any language when translating an article for this Wikipedia. See [POLICY] for more information. Other language Wikipedias operate independently and may have different policies, you should check each language Wikipedia's policies before translating in to other Wikipedias.

question_text: From the English version of the website, can I also edit the Italian version?
reply_text: The English and Italian versions are edited separately, but you can edit either page with the same account. Your account will import to any other languages or projects you edit on.

question_text: Hi, I dont know how many edits I have to make before I can upload a translation. Also, do I have to be an autoconfirmed user to be able to publish translations?
reply_text: Hello @, I'd hope you can find what you need by reading [POLICY]. Cheers!

question_text: Hello i have a couple questions how can i put up a new page for someone famous in my country locally and i think he deserves a page and do i have to write in English because i know arabic more thanks!
reply_text:
Hi @. Before you get into writing new articles, I'd strongly recommend that you get some experience of just making edits to existing pages first: take a look at the [POLICY]. However, if you're really keen to write an article from scratch, read through [HELP_PAGE] first, then use the [POLICY] to guide you through the process. Some general suggestions from me:
The most important thing is to gather the sources from which you'll write the article . Don't be tempted to write the article up front and find the sources to back it up, because [POLICY].
Very generally, you will need [POLICY] sources with [POLICY] of the subject to demonstrate that the subject is sufficiently [POLICY] to warrant its own article. There's useful information about how to choose good sources and avoid bad sources .
Make sure you use your own words to write the article - don't copy and paste from your sources, because [POLICY] are taken seriously on Wikipedia.
Don't use a [POLICY] or other kind of AI to generate an article - they don't do a good job at all, and your article will probably end up getting deleted.
Use [POLICY] or to draft your article, rather than create it first off in mainspace. That will allow you to take your time. When it's ready, just move it to mainspace.
As for what language to write in, you must use English on English Wikipedia. If you are more fluent in Arabic, you might find it easier contributing to [WIKILINK]. Cheers,

question_text: Hello Iffy, I am editing a Norwegian artist's Wikipedia in Norwegian and would like to make an English one as well. Should I edit the Norwegian one first and then translate it into English? Also, I help manage the artists website and have a COI regarding the editing. I am mainly adding exhibitions and dates and images. Is this ok?
reply_text: Hello! I don't know what the rules are for COI editors on the Norwegian Wikipedia, so I can't help you there. If you are considering translating the article for the English Wikipedia, I strongly recommend that you do so by creating a draft page and use the [POLICY] process, so that independent editors can review whether the subject of the article and the article's content meets this Wikipedia's guidelines. As for exhibitions, date and images, I would focus more on what [POLICY] say about the subject's work rather than trying to create a gallery or a comprehensive listing of the subject's works. Images also need to either be freely licensed (and therefore can be uploaded to Wikimedia Commons) or qualify as [POLICY].

question_text: how can i be allowed to translate
reply_text: Hello, Bonani Bless. As a new editor, you have a lot to learn about how to create an acceptable new article on the English Wikipedia. Whether writing a completely new article or translating an article from another language, you need to comply with the [POLICY] of the English Wikipedia. Read and study [POLICY]. Your new account cannot create new articles directly or use semi-automated translating tools yet. But you can manually translate and submit drafts to the [POLICY] process. Read [POLICY] and [[[POLICY] for guidance on translating to and from English.

question_text: Can I use a different language Wikipedia article as my source when translating or how do i translate if not
reply_text: Hello! [POLICY], so you can't cite Wikipedia articles. However, you are allowed to just cite the same sources that the foreign language article cites. Keep in mind that English Wikipedia often has much stricter rules than other language Wikipedias about what sources are acceptable and what topics even deserve an article—not everything you may find on a foreign Wikipedia can be added to English Wikipedia. You can see [POLICY] for more information on translating articles.

question_text: Hello! I'm extremely new to wiki editing, and I was wondering about translating articles into a language I'm not fully fluent in myself. reply_text: Hi @! Each language edition of Wikipedia is run by its own community, so I would ask at that Wikipedia about whether they would welcome such translation efforts or not. (Related: ) Cheers,

question_text: I am a scientist and I would like to make an article about a physicist I work with. He has done some important work over the years but I don't think he's won any rewards or had any articles written about him. Wikipedia's guidelines say that self-promotion does not count as good enough to be notable for a page. Would papers that he has published count as self-promotion or would they still qualify considering the scientific and professional nature of the content?
reply_text: The basic guidelines are on the page [POLICY]. Unfortunately, our leading expert in notability guidelines for academics, DGG passed away from time ago, and I personally still miss asking him questions like this. The only other regular editor who I think knows something about these guidelines is MelanieN. In general, if your colleague only has published papers, it would be possible but difficult to have an article - it really depends how widespread and influential those papers are, and how frequently they are cited by other papers as an outstanding work that is an essential reference.

question_text: Hi! I've been trying to create a page about a Victorian botanist who discovered a rare fern which is named after her. My first issue was that I didn't include enough citations (now hopefully fixed!) but I've now been told that it's not notable enough for inclusion. I'm not sure what to do about that because that feels like a matter of perspective - she discovered a new fern hybrid that's named after her - that's amazing! It's more than her husband ever did, who just seemed to get born into a noble family and then die one day. What can I do? The page is: [LINK]
reply_text: Hello LetsGetBotanical the advice from the reviewer is to add more citations. If there is enough coverage about the subject from reliable published sources then that should establish notability. You should also add each citation inline at the end of the supported text. Then if you find enough sources to support every statement in the article, you can try resubmitting.

reply_text: Hi there @, sorry for the late reply. Are there any [POLICY] like newspapers or books by reputable authors that report on your distant relative? To be considered for inclusion in Wikipedia, articles need to have [POLICY] of their subject, which is more than just passing mentions. You don't need to complete an editing workshop before you can add content, you can just start out with the [POLICY] to help guide you through the creation process. But do make sure that your relative is covered by these reliable sources before creating a draft article.

question_text:
Hello,
I'm looking to edit a local politician's wiki (also a family member) who's information is sorely lacking. I was wondering if I could cite county records and ancestry.com when discussing this person's early life.
reply_text:
:DreadPirateRoberts1 Hi, and welcome to Wikipedia! First of all, I would strongly caution you against making edits to the article of a person whom you have a family connection with; see [POLICY] for more info on that front. Regarding the sources you mentioned, county records may be ok if used with caution. Wikipedia tries to stick to [POLICY], as [POLICY] are generally less reliable. See also [POLICY], which states that primary sources like county records should not be used to support personal details (e.g., date of birth). Wikipedia has a blurb about Ancestry's reliability [POLICY] - the consensus is that it is generally unreliable, and thus should not be used as a source.

question_text:
Hello,
My name is Soltan Adel, and I am the creator and founder behind Designed by Soltan, a family of mobile applications. When creating my first article, I found it difficult to compile the information while having to cite it, considering I'm the primary source. I also had it immediately deleted for that reason. Not to mention, it was also difficult to upload an image relating to the app without an error from the "Wikipedia Commons" telling me to upload an image taken with a camera. As this is my first time making an article on Wikipedia, there is lots to learn. Can you please help me resolve all of these issues I have run into? I appreciate your time and effort.
Regards,
Soltan Adel
reply_text: Soltanon67 welcome to Wikipedia! Unfortunately, I would strongly recommend against creating an article about a company you are involved with. Wikipedia is written by volunteers and to maintain a [POLICY] we are typically free from both financial and personal relationships to the topics we write about. We have to disclose conflicts of interest and follow [POLICY]. It is your responsibility to read and comply with the conflict of interest guidelines. Additionally, creating a new article is one of the hardest tasks on Wikipedia and requires experience in lots of different skills. Almost all company articles people try to write are complete non-starters because the subjects do not meet our "[POLICY]" requirements—those of being covered in-depth by multiple reliable, independent sources. This means that no amount of hard work, skill or time invested will lead to Wikipedia hosting an article on the topic, as it is outside our deliberately limited scope (to make unpaid volunteers' maintenance work manageable and protect the site's reputation). All information in Wikipedia must be [POLICY] to a secondary source. If you are the primary source then it means the information is of no interest to Wikipedia, a tertiary source encyclopedia. Most images also cannot be uploaded to Wikimedia Commons as this requires you to be the copyright holder (the person who made the image) and to release it under a free (e.g. CC-BY SA 4.0) license. In summary, almost no newcomers succeed in creating a new article before gaining experience elsewhere, and Wikipedia is not interested in promoting your company or sharing information that has not already garnered attention in secondary sources. If you have any follow-up questions, please let me know! —

question_text: Hello! I'm absolutely new here. I expect my contributions to be limited and focused. I've made one edit (before I set up an account) in which I added parents to my uncle's page ([LINK] I am active in genealogy ([LINK] and I anticipate that any activity I will have on Wikipedia will stem from that interest. I'm not clear on how citation standard differ in Wikipedia from what I'm used to. Comments and guidance are welcome! Best, Chris
reply_text:
Hi SailorChris1951 and welcome to Wikipedia.
There are several different citation templates that editors can choose from; the list [POLICY].
For information regarding citing sources in general, see [POLICY].
Never use self-published sources, e.g., FamilySearch; more about that [POLICY].
The article you mentioned, [WIKILINK], is a living person, so [POLICY] is the policy and other information regarding biographies of living people.
Looking closely at the reference which added parents to BL's article, I do not see where it indicates that those people are his parents. Adding information that you know but without including a reliable source to verify it is considered to be "original research", which is prohibited; more about that [POLICY]. Perhaps there's an interview someplace which states that those people are his parents; I would add that reference and remove the FamilySearch one.
Editing articles about our relatives (unless they are dead) denotes a perceived conflict of interest, so it's best to avoid doing so, but it you do so, the editor must declare the conflict of interest; more info [POLICY].
Lastly, I left a with information for new editors which should help you get started.
Glad that you're here, SailorChris1951, and you're always welcome to ask more questions. I'm still asking them, even after 18 years and >300K edits.

question_text: Hi Soni, I wanted to add another person's image with a CC-BY-SA licence and I've been told the correct attribution, but reading all the rules here and on the alternative route via wikimedia it seems that's maybe not allowed? reply_text:
Hello @,
By default, the person who holds the license for any image is the photographer (Unless they explicitly give the copyright to someone else). And [LINK] require the photo to be at least CC-by-SA or similar. This requires the copyright holder to themselves release the photo under that license.
Copyright holders can usually either upload the photo to commons themselves, or send an email to [POLICY] similarly releasing the copyright. Those are generally the ways to upload any photo here unless it explicitly falls under [POLICY] (I don't think yours does).
Hope this helps, please feel free to ping me again if you have any other questions

question_text: Hello! I hope you're doing well, i wanted to ask if Wikipedia enforces strict grammatical standards for contributions. If so, what kind of linguistic choice should I adhere to when editing?
reply_text: Schorensaw Welcome to Wikipedia. I don't think any strict grammatical standards is enforced for contributions. Some grammatical errors crop up from time to time in any editor's contributions, and other editors are always free to fix those issues. That said, if someone's contributions are completely unintelligible, then we politely ask them to continue contributions to the language Wikipedia that they are more familiar with, instead of the English wiki. Hope this helps. If not, please feel free to ask a more specific question. Happy editing!

question_text:
Hello,
My name is Gonzalo Castellón. I recently created this account because I’ve been tasked by Dr. Alfredo Sfeir-Younis to work on significantly improving and updating his Wikipedia page.
Before making any major edits, I want to make sure I fully understand Wikipedia’s guidelines so that my contributions are appropriate, well-sourced, and not misinterpreted as vandalism or promotional content. My goal is to ensure the page is accurate, neutral, and aligned with Wikipedia standards.
Could you please advise me on best practices for editing a biography under these circumstances, especially regarding potential conflict of interest and how to proceed transparently?
Thank you in advance for your guidance.
Best regards,
Gonzalo Castellón
reply_text: If you want to edit with a conflict of interest, I recommend reading the [POLICY] page. I'll just tell you the main points. On your userpage, you should put [WIKILINK]. On [WIKILINK], you should put [WIKILINK] each time you want to make an edit. Please do not directly edit the page. It may take some time for a volunteer to respond to your request, so just keep that in mind. Each time you create an edit request, you want to make sure that all information complies with Wikipedia's sourcing standards, [POLICY]. Most major English-language sources are listed at [POLICY], where their reliability is assessed. Most of the time, you will be citing [POLICY] sources (those not directly connected to the subject themself), and if you are citing [POLICY] sources, then you have to follow some extra rules (which are listed at [POLICY]). You can check [POLICY] for more information on citations, which are very important. Essentially, when you are editing, there is a "Cite" button that you can click. After you click that, a bar will pop up beneath the top bar, on the left there will be a "Templates" section that you can click and it gives you various templates for various different types of sources. You just need to fill in the information there and it will create a citation for you. You should also read the [POLICY] section and Wikipedia's policy on [POLICY]. If you have any questions you may ask me, or you may also ask at the [POLICY] ', EasternShah

question_text:
Hello, Encoded!
Thank you for volunteering to be my mentor. I feel like one of those people who call into talk shows saying "long time listener, first time caller", as I've used Wikipedia a zillion times, but have never even thought about editing it. However, due to several changed circumstances, I'm now interested in doing so. So, thank you in advance for your advice.
Some of the editing is related to my myriad interests in history, literature, sports, etc. Some is related to my work, for which I understand there are a distinct set of rules; i.e. I'll need to declare a "conflict of interest".
I think I can figure out the former fairly quickly, but we'll see if that's true! It's the latter about which I'm more uncertain.
Can you please point me to written guidelines for such editing? Beyond that, do you have any suggestions for how to proceed?
Thank you!
reply_text:
Hey @, welcome to Wikipedia! I've just placed a welcome message on with a bunch of links with information and tutorials on it.
The full conflict of interest policy can be found here: [POLICY]. With regards to your work if you're editing an article about your work, research, or something else you directly contribute to outside of Wikipedia, or one of your competitors, that would trigger a COI. However, if it's something you work with you can still edit pages about the general idea (For example: A baker can't edit articles about their own bakery or a competitor without following the guidelines, but they can edit an article about bread). I'd recommend reading the above linked page to check!
On articles you do have a COI with I recommend using the [POLICY] to suggest changes to articles and [POLICY] if you're making any new ones. You can absolutely still contribute to wikipedia with a conflict of interest, your edits will just need to be reviewed. If you're editing articles that you do not have a COI with, you can edit as normal. You'll need to disclose any conflicts (see [POLICY] for info) I recommend keeping a list of pages/topics on your that you have a conflict with - this counts as the disclosure.
Other than that, I'd recommend starting off with the [POLICY] which teaches you all you need to know about Wikipedia. You could edit an article that you're interested in, or visit the [POLICY] which contains a list of tasks that need to be done around the site.
Hope this helps, if you have any questions at all please feel free to send me a message!

question_text:
Hi
I've noticed a number of articles in the pop sector that point to a usurped domain name as the "original link". Archived links points to correct archive but should I be reporting the original link as usurped? All of them are going to the same domain.
reply_text: Hi! Thank you for getting in touch, this is a really good question! If the citations in question are formatted using citation templates, url-status=usurped (or, if the new content of the link is problematic, eg. malicious content, gambling, etc., url-status=unfit). This will mean that the template prioritises the archived link, and suppresses the original URL so that the original link isn't clickable. For example:will produce: I hope this helps! If you have any more questions then please feel free to ask, and I'd be happy to have a look at the articles in question if you're not sure about anything. :)

question_text: Hi, I am new to editing. Initially I just want to remove a hyperlink between person A and person B. They are not the same person. When you publish changes, it'll ask for an edit summary, where you can add an explanation for your changes, like "removed a link to incorrect person".
Happy editing! ~ ~

reply_text: Hello! It's always important to add an edit summary so other users can see what changes you made at a glance. reply_text: @ Plagiarism is a big no-no. It would be considered copyright infringement, which creates legal and reputational issues for Wikipedia. Small issues may result in a warning, but if it becomes pervasive, it would result in a ban. Though we appreciate all users who want to contribute, the contributions must be original. Make sure to always put things in your own words or to quote when that is not possible.

question_text: Hello Marchjuly! I am wondering how long do I have to be an editor to start translating articles between languages? thank you!
reply_text:
@ It is not the length of time that is the limiting factor. It is your competence. If your competence is greater than your desire that it is time to discover what we require here. An article in 'Fooian' may be acceptable for the 'Fooian Language Wikipedia', but the acceptance criteria for the English Language Wikipedia are the toughest of all language versions.
Thus you must be fluent in both languagges and also understand and apply all policies and procedures.
You are early in your career here, and have not yet shown that you are more than a newcomer. We all had to start as newcomers, so there is no harm in that. I suggest you become familiar with this place before looking for the rules about translation and attribution 🇺🇦 🇺🇦
Thank you very much for your response! I agree that it is vital to protect pages in the language most used throughout the site in check. I can't really prove it to you now can I, but I have the skills and experience to become a volunteer translator. In any case, do you reckon it's easier for me to get accepted to translate articles from English into Slovak (my native language)? Thanks so much for your help and kindness to show a newcomer how to contribute to a great cause! I'm sure this is the beginning of my journey!
Hi Nejakytypco. Perhaps Timtrent who answered your question above will respond to this one as well, but everyone editing Wikipedia is a [WIKILINK] so to speak;so, there are no official Wikipedia translators per se. As long as you're language skills are sufficient and you're able to do so in accordance with [WIKILINK] or [WIKILINK], you should be OK. It's really important to understand, though, that [WIKILINK] if the article you're tying to create is about a subject not deemed to be [WIKILINK]. There are many Wikipedias besides English Wikipedia and they're all separate projects without their own respective policies and guidelines, and their own respective communities; since they're sort of all members of the same family, there's lots of similarities but there can also be some important differences. So, [WIKILINK]. English Wikipedia, for example, has more around seven million articles; while many of these are quite good, there are also many that aren't good at all and perhaps even shouldn't have been created in the first place. Even though English Wikipedia is the largest of all the Wikipedias and its community, for the most part, tends to be more attentive to applying its various policies and guidelines, there are still lots of articles created each day that probably shouldn't be created; finding these articles and nominating them for deletion can sometimes take time, sometimes a long time, and usually there's a bit of luck involved. Other language Wikipedias almost surely have similar issues, but also tend to have much fewer volunteer editors trying to separate the good from the bad. Before you start translating any English Wikipedia articles into Slovak Wikipedia articles, you probably should familiarize yourself with Slovak Wikipedia's policies and guidelines, particularly its notability guidelines. You don't want spend lots of time translating an article that's has a good chance of ending up deleted. You also might want to do some editing on Slovak Wikipedia (even if it's just cleaning up existing articles) so that you get a feel for its policies and guidelines, how it wants articles to be formatted and how members of its community interact with one another. Anything you do on Slovak Wikipedia is going to need to be done in accordance with its policies and guidelines, and any assistance you need on Slovak Wikipedia is going to need to be provided by its community; so, being able to effectively communicate, read and write in Slovak will probably make things much easier for you. Questions about English Wikipedia can be answered on English Wikipedia, but questions about Slovak Wikipedia are going to need to be asked there.

question_text: hi! i am new to editing. how large are the edits i can make as a new editor? can i add new sections, or reorder existing sections? thank you so so much
reply_text: Welcome to wikipedia, @! I'll drop some helpful links on your user talk page. There's no limit to the size or impact of your edits, technically speaking. However, if you're making a bunch of different changes, many editors prefer to make them in multiple steps. This way, if someone objects to one of the changes you made, but not the others, it's easier for them to revert just that change. Also, since there's no way to save your work other than by publishing it, if you're paranoid about losing what you've written, you'll want to publish in smaller chunks. Personally, if I'm rearranging sections on an article, I'll make an edit that is only that, and leave text changes for a subsequent edit. If I'm writing new text, I tend to stop and publish every paragraph or two.

question_text: Hello, when can I start writing a new article?
reply_text:
, to answer your question, you can start writing a new article whenever you want.
However, note that you can't create an article directly in 'mainspace', or where viewers see it. Instead, there is a technical restriction which only allows you to create a new article directly when you are [POLICY] - that is, your account has existed for four complete days and made ten edits.
Furthermore, creating a new article is one of the most difficult things you could possibly do on Wikipedia, only made even more arduous since you are a new editor. This would be akin to attending a chess competition having just learned what the rules are.
If your mind is set on creating a new article, I highly recommend you spend some time (at least a few days) making edits suggested at , then reading [POLICY] very carefully and creating a draft. Also note that creating articles is generally not the best way you can contribute to Wikipedia.
Lastly, if you are being [POLICY] to edit Wikipedia or have a [POLICY] with your planned article subject, then you should read the relevant guideline/policy, which are linked earlier in the sentence. Happy editing!

question_text: How do i create a valid artice name?.
reply_text:
Hello Smadur1997,
Creating a valid article name on Wikipedia is an important step in contributing to this vast knowledge base. Here are some guidelines to help you:
# Concise and Clear: The article name should clearly and concisely communicate what a user may find in the article.
# Avoid Descriptive Adjectives: Try to keep the article name short and avoid using descriptive adjectives.
# Unique: Make sure the article name is unique. You can check this by searching for the name on Wikipedia before creating your article.
# Follow Wikipedia’s Naming Conventions: Wikipedia has specific naming conventions that you should follow. You can find more information about this on the [POLICY] page.
Remember, the goal is to make the article easily discoverable and understandable to readers. If you need further assistance, don’t hesitate to ask.

reply_text: Hi @ and welcome to Wikipedia. (My name is Mike Turnbull, not Mr Murshid). You appear to be in danger of starting an [POLICY] at [WIKILINK] because you are insisting on a particular version of the content which an experienced editor, believes is your personal commentary. If it were, that's not allowed since Wikipedia does not publish [POLICY]. The correct thing for you to do now is to discuss the issue with other editors on the talk page of the article, which I see that you have begun to do. This is standard procedure summarised at [POLICY]: the aim is to achieve [POLICY], if necessary after possible [POLICY] . I have no wish to become involved in that process but can advise you on Wikipedia's norms. One minor point I can immediately see in your text is that you use phrases like "[QUOTE] but if we want to research the [/QUOTE]" (my emphasis). This is against Wikipedia's [POLICY], which says that [QUOTE] an article should never refer to its editors or readers using I, my, we, us, our, or similar words [/QUOTE]. I also suggest that you read the essay [POLICY].

reply_text: Welcome to Wikipedia editing Kelebaser! I recommend you take a look at the various [POLICY] to understand how different types of debates, looking especially at those related to biographies, are held and hopefully eventually resolved. Best,

question_text: Hello, I made a factual edit to an article and it was later reverted, and I disagree with the reasoning given for the reversion. What is the proper way to dispute the reversion? I made the same edit again and explained why I believe the reversion was a mistake in my edit description, but I am not sure if that is the best way.
reply_text: Hello! The most common method to resolve disputes such as this one is to follow the [POLICY] process. After your edit has been reverted, the next step in the BRD process is to discuss the matter with those who disagree with you. Edit summaries are one way to discuss things, but the best place to discuss contentious changes to articles is on the [HELP_PAGE] of the article.

question_text:
Hello
I've joined WP with the initial intention of editing just one page ('Selectron tube'). I have extensive material to contribute to this page.
I am a research scientist and have published many peer-reviewed journal articles in physics. I feel that I can adequately write technical prose.
That said, I am new here and I am uncertain of the editing 'ethos'. When i make edits, are past authors notified? Is there a way to share draft edits with past authors to get their thoughts - or do we just jump in, publish my changes, and let past authors edit the live document?
Many thanks for the mentorship.
reply_text:
@ anyone who wants to be notified of changes to an article can put it on their [POLICY], but past authors aren't notified automatically. If you want, you can ask for thoughts on proposed edits on the [WIKILINK], but except for controversial edits on high-traffic pages the standard thing is to just make the change and let people revert or discuss afterward if there are issues. There's an essay about this at [POLICY].
:Do note though that Wikipedia style is pretty different from technical journal articles. You might want to take a look at [POLICY] or [POLICY] for some examples of physics-related articles that have been reviewed by the community.

question_text:
Hi, I'm new to editing Wikipedia and have lots of questions. Thank you for being my mentor. For now I'm limit it to two: recently I was making some suggested edits and came across an article for "The Master of the Arboga alterpiece. There is no article for the Arboga master piece, and from what I could tell after 30 minutes of poking around, there is nothing commonly called by this name. There is a church in Sweden that has an altarpiece that came from the area this article claims the Master worked in, but that's as much as I've found. Given that there is no article for the Alterpiece itself, it seems strange to have an article about the unknown artist. Is this something that should be marked for deletion? if so, what is the process for that? Here is the link to the article: [LINK]
2. I have a graduate degree in Shakespeare studies and have put together many annotated bibliographies on diverse topics related to Shakespeare. I want to help edit articles using that information/knowledge, and many of those articles have are protected. What are the best practices for requested an edit to a page like this? I have used the talk page on the Macbeth article (yesterday), but I want to make sure I'm going out about this in the proper/respectful way. Thank you!
reply_text:
@, on the first, does [WIKILINK] help at all? Looks like we have two sources about them in German. But quite old. It's also possible that this is fully invented and those sources don't exist, though this would be an odd kind of article to fake. Normally, I would refer you to [POLICY] in this kind of case (ie, a mostly abandoned-looking stub that you can't find sources for), but since there is an article on de-wiki, its deletion wouldn't be "uncontroversial", and the appropriate process would be [POLICY]. Before you tried taking it there, you'd want to establish that the sources on the German article either don't verify the text or don't say much about the artist anyway. Since only a single page is being cited for either of the relevant sources, my guess is that there isn't much out there and it may be only a brief mention.
As for the articles being protected, I'd bet they're only semi-protected, which means that, as of the edit you just made to my talk page, you're able to edit them. So go ahead!

question_text: Someone is already deleting my edits because they say my edit was unsourced. The legislation is itself the source and that legislation has a detailed citation on the page. Especially when the longer standing disclosure on the page (that either is equally OK or equally flawed) was left intact. I think the existing provision was adequately sourced because it was a description of the legislation (which is a very legitimate source) and I think my addition was equally adequately sourced.
reply_text: @ I note that after you posted this on my talk page you have had a fairly extensive discussion with the other editor on your own talk page. That is the correct way to proceed, although it is usually better to have article-specific discussions on the talk page of the article in question so that other [POLICY] will see it and can chip in to help form a [POLICY]. As it happens, [WIKILINK] has <30 watchers, so you may need to use Wikipedia's various forms of [POLICY]. bear in mind that it is usually fun to find another editor who is as interested in a topic as you are and you need to [POLICY], which in this context means focusing on the content of their edits and yours and never on their motivations or conduct. Wikipedia is built on the [POLICY] cycle and what you have run into is absolutely standard practice.

question_text:
Hello there! You are my assigned mentor.
a NPOV tag under a specific section of an article (Heterodox Academy) was readded but a editor who watches the page reverted it and said « Nope ».
Am I correct to figure that because the tag was anonymously removed without discussion with the editors involved, that it should remain until the disagreement is resolved?
The article’s main editors I see have said they are against the organization, which I fear creates a POV version of the article. I’ve seen previous editors have tried and failed to resolve this POV dilemma. I have not edited because of this track record.
Looking for your experienced advice. Kindly,
reply_text: Hi! Without getting into formal policy, my advice in this situation would be to not reinstate the tag, but instead open a new discussion on the talk page, lay out your reasons for believing the tag should be re-added, and tag the editor who removed it asking them to explain their reasons. Try to come to an agreement with that editor. If normal converstion doesn't resolve the issue, you could look into [POLICY], but I strongly recommend having a change you want made to the article that's more specific than "retain this NPOV tag", since imo having a big argument over a tag by itself is basically never construction.

question_text: Hey Geoff, I was wondering if I could create a wiki on myself and are there any restrictions or special rules to go by?
reply_text:
GilbertKuah Wikipedia is a collaborative encyclopedia written by its readers. The subjects for our articles are generally chosen by the editing community rather than by request. While there is a venue where you can request articles, a request for an article creation on oneself is likely to be declined. You can visit our 'requests for articles' page at [POLICY].
It's also important to realise that Wikipedia strongly discourages articles written by the subjects themselves or by others close to a subject because of the difficulty in writing objectively about yourself, your family, or your work. Please see [POLICY] for our guidelines on writing about yourself or topics you are personally involved with. While keeping in mind conflict of interest issues, please note that if someone does create an article about you, you should feel more than welcome to edit the article's Talk page to ensure that each piece of information is factual and complying with our "Biography of Living Persons" policy. This policy can be viewed at [POLICY].
You may also like to read [POLICY] for a guideline on the type of articles that are generally accepted.

Sorry for the newbie questions! Just trying to understand what options are available.
Thank you! Jen
reply_text:
Hi! If you're working on a page about your grandfather, you may want to take a look at the [POLICY]. You are welcome to make minor fixes (grammar, phrasing, etc) yourself, but at least on the English Wikipedia it's highly recommended to make an edit request if making significant changes to the article of someone you're related to. German Wikipedia may have different policies, I'm not quite sure. Make sure to have sources for everything-- even if you personally know it's true, we can't have it on Wikipedia unless it's [POLICY].
If you want to make an article on the English Wikipedia, you can translate the German article and submit it though [POLICY], where a more experinced editor will review it and make sure it's following all the relevant conflict of interest and sourcing guidelines. Make sure to note that you're translating the German version somewhere.

question_text: When is it appropriate to delete something? How much evidence do you need that it isn't real?
reply_text:
If evidence isn’t real, then it is usually fine to delete it. Try excluding fake evidence.
@: If you can't find sources to that information, you can either remove it as part of [POLICY] process or tag it with [TEMPLATE_REF] template at the end of the statement. If you remove it, you can wait for someone to come by, reverting it and add sources which are valid. If it is not valid, you can engage them in a discussion on the talk page. The second way of putting a citation needed tag is similar, just that you are highlight that the statement requires a source. I would usually wait for a couple of months before removing the statement if you choose to go through this way.
Either method works. And if the sources are still unbelievable, or false, you can escalate upwards to get the attention of other editors to look into this, and if necessary, have some administrative actions taken.

question_text: Hello, what is meant by minor edit? and why is that important? thank you

It’s important because it keeps the edit history clearer, signals that the change is small, and avoids bothering other editors with notifications.

question_text: Hi, I have a question: How do I know I myself should edit an article? reply_text: Hi @! You can edit an article directly pretty much any time. Theoretically, you could leave a note in the talk page, or alert the relevant wiki project, but those are often not watched. Make sure you have a [POLICY] for the information and then [POLICY] and go right ahead. A wiki functions on people making the changes they think are best, and then other people correcting them, and then other people correcting them, and so on and on, so don't worry about being perfect, just better than what was there before.

question_text:
Hey there,
I made an update and it doesn't look like anything was changed after but I noticed it was added to a "filter log" which claims it could be "promotion"... no actions were taken, but just a little confused and wondering if I did something wrong / what to do next time / if I need to change anything for this one.
reply_text:
Welcome to Wikipedia, Squeak41, and I apologize for my late reply. The [POLICY] is a community maintained set of [WIKILINK] filters that either blocks or logs for review edits which meet certain criteria, usually to stop edits which are unambiguously disruptive (e.g one filter is set to block attempts to add the phrase "yo mama" to articles) or which meet patterns that are consistent with [POLICY].
As far as I'm aware, the filter being triggered - especially if no action was taken - is not inherently a bad thing. I'm not much more knowledgable with filters aside from what I've said above, so I don't know if I can access your triggering of the relevant filter specifically. I'd say you are good to just continue editing as normal, presuming it remains [POLICY]. question_text:
Hello Ultraodan,
This is it's nice to meet you!
I’ve submitted my first draft, [DRAFT], for review under the Articles for Creation process. I noticed one reviewer has already made a small edit, and I’m learning as I go.
Since this is my first time creating a biography, I would be very grateful if you could take a look or share any advice on how to improve the draft to meet the review standards.
Thank you so much for your time and guidance!
— K55D11
reply_text:
Hi @, that looks like a good start. I can't do everything I would normally do in a review simply because I can't read the Chinese sources.
Looking at the English sources, press releases are not [POLICY]. Anything published by his company is not [POLICY] either so those can't be used for more than basic facts, not to establish notability. I recommend going through your sources and checking that they all meet the standards for reliability and independence.
For the actual structure, you've got a lot of [POLICY] that shouldn't be in the body of the article. Instead these could be [POLICY] for articles that can be created in the future. The section headings should be in sentence case, not title case, there's an explanation at [POLICY].
There's a few things that make me wonder if you used artificial intelligence to help you with this draft. If you did, any article would be much better if it is written by a human.
That's a bit of advice, take your time to go through it. You might get more or different feedback in an actual AfC review. You'll need to be patient for a review as there is a very long back-log. I can see this draft taking longer to get one only because it is longer and has a lot of sources (making it take longer to review) and some of the sources aren't in English (making it harder to review). As always, if you have any questions feel free to ask me!

question_text: Hello there! I am trying to fix my citations for the approval of Lake George Music Festival as an article. Could I have some help? I cannot tell what is wrong with my page and why it is getting declined.
reply_text: AVAcados072105 About [DRAFT] which has been reviewed and rejected by two different reviewers. Their notes point you in the direction of what you need to do to improve the article. I recommend following each of the blue links and carefully reading the articles which are linked. An issue cited by both reviewers is the lack of [POLICY]. For example, a reviewer mentioned that the article subjects' publications are not sufficient. And as another an example, blogs and publications that anyone can edit, such as [LINK] are not considered reliable because anyone can edit. (By definition, this includes Wikipedia itself.) The other important comment is that the draft reads more like an advertisement for the festival than an encyclopedia article. You need to change the tone where it reads like a promotion to more of an objective description. This is part of the difficulty involved in writing about your employer. (Good that you have disclosed as a paid editor per the [POLICY].)
